1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff2b199/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5137x2883+0+271/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2F0c%2Fae45a1ff4383816fa2f0ed35d319%2Frapidresponse-09xx25-04.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9d08aaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5137x2883+0+271/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2F0c%2Fae45a1ff4383816fa2f0ed35d319%2Frapidresponse-09xx25-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Cristóbal Cavazos of Casa DuPage Workers Center, who drives around to respond to alerts about immigration enforcement activity." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9d08aaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5137x2883+0+271/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2F0c%2Fae45a1ff4383816fa2f0ed35d319%2Frapidresponse-09xx25-04.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6f5a3df/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5137x2883+0+271/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2F0c%2Fae45a1ff4383816fa2f0ed35d319%2Frapidresponse-09xx25-04.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9d08aaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5137x2883+0+271/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2F0c%2Fae45a1ff4383816fa2f0ed35d319%2Frapidresponse-09xx25-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cristóbal Cavazos of Casa DuPage Workers Center drives arund Chicago’s west suburbs in 2025, responding to alerts about immigration enforcement activity.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Faster alert system</h3><p>Community leaders and rapid-responders say they are seeing more targeted arrests rather than the large, highly visible sweeps seen last fall.</p><p>Cristóbal Cavazos, executive director of Casa DuPage Workers Center, an organization that documents immigration enforcement in the west suburbs, said the shift makes it harder for volunteers to track enforcement operations and get there quickly when they hear of them.</p><p>Cavazos said he’s seen as many as seven agents trying to arrest one person. That means people who weren't the original targets but are nearby might also get arrested.</p><p>Last year, Cavazos said he helped organize about 300 volunteer rapid-responders, who patrolled in Elgin, Villa Park and other suburbs. He said they used WhatsApp and then Signal messages to send people alerts about immigration enforcement. Those tools were helpful but not as practical when federal agents were spotted and rapid-responders needed to sound the alarm in real time, according to Cavazos, who said that, at times, the chats had too many comments, which slowed response times.</p><p>To alert people more quickly, Cavazos said his team is moving to a dispatch system using cellphone apps. Responders use the apps to provide live updates from their location, aiming to cut response times from the current 10 to 15 minutes to as few as five.</p><p>Cavazos got the idea for the dispatch system from the time he spent volunteering as a rapid-responder in Minneapolis in January. </p><p>U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents "would be there, and people would be [on location] within one or two minutes,” Cavazos said. “They were just all over ICE like a cheap suit. That's community power.”</p><p>Some rapid-responders also are focusing on a narrower, block-by-block strategy to speed their responses to reports of immigration enforcement. Guiracocha said the aim is to use hyperlocal text chains and group chats for people to instantly alert their neighbors.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-620000" name="html-embed-module-620000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28855661"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28855661/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="chart visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p></p><h3>More training</h3><p><i>“Migra</i> watch” and “know your rights” training sessions have been around for years, but such sessions grew in popularity and evolved as federal enforcement escalated last fall.</p><p>Previously, a lot of the focus had been on showing people how to document an arrest or to ask for a warrant. Now, the training also includes planning for what to do after someone is arrested and taken to a detention center. </p><p>Data analyzed by WBEZ and the Sun-Times shows 1,341 people who were detained in the Chicago area from the start of Operation Midway Blitz through mid-March voluntarily agreed to self-deport. </p><p>Cavazos said he wants people to know they don't have to self-deport.</p><p>“Don’t talk, don’t sign, ask to speak to a lawyer,” is the advice Cavazos gives. His training sessions cover <i>habeas corpus</i> petitions, a legal process used to challenge the legality of a detention.</p><p>Participants also learn about the Castañon-Nava case, a 2022 settlement restricting federal immigration agents' authority to make warrantless arrests in Illinois and five other states. Agents are supposed to have probable cause that someone is in the country unlawfully and provide evidence that the person is a flight risk. Recent violations of this court order led a federal judge to order the release of immigrants who were illegally detained and 343 people have benefited from the case.</p><p>“Even up to now, people are getting released,” Guiracocha said. “We see those stories, and we put them in the training to say, this is why this matters.”</p><h3>Training to face aggressive tactics <b> </b></h3><p>Organizers said they try to convey the level of violence that immigrants and activists could face by role playing difficult arrest scenarios and teaching new deescalation tactics.</p><p>Those "really shifted from ‘these are the things that you can do if you see ICE’ to ‘ICE is unpredictable, and your safety is the No. 1 priority,’ ” said Rey Wences, senior director of deportation defense for the Illinois Coalition for Immigrant and Refugee Rights.</p><p>Wences said participants in training are told to follow protocols if shots are fired. They also learn how to protect themselves against chemical agents like tear gas and pepper spray, with an emphasis on using personal protective equipment. The training also shows people how to create an emergency plan in case they get hurt, arrested or deported.</p><p>During simulations, role-play participants act as agents and shout aggressive remarks to test a volunteer’s ability to remain firm without escalating the situation.</p><p>Some trainers describe the sessions as a “taste” of reality because it mirrors the chaos and crises that unfold when immigrants are being arrested. The sessions conclude with volunteers talking about their mental and emotional reactions to foster awareness and help people stay focused in case they find themselves facing aggressive tactics.</p><p>“There is no limit [to] the violence that these agents and this administration has on the community,” Guiracocha said. "I think [that] has been an awful lesson for us to learn but something that we can now use to prepare to equip ourselves."</p><p><i>Contributing: Lauren FitzPatrick, Alden Loury</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/29/standing-up-to-ice-in-suburban-chicago-the-peoples-patrol-puts-its-faith-in-resistance" >Standing up to ICE in the suburbs, the People’s Patrol puts its faith in resistance</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/12/28/broadview-detainees-operation-midway-blitz-self-deporting-high-rates-analysis-finds" >Broadview detainees during Operation Midway Blitz were self-deporting at alarming rates, analysis finds</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/05/volunteers-on-ice-patrol-in-pilsen-looking-for-agents" >Volunteers on ICE patrol in Pilsen, looking for agents</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Adriana Cardona-Maguigad |  WBEZ

entries.title: Edgewater's historic Bryn Mawr Avenue heads toward possible landmark status
entries.summary: <p>A three-block stretch of Edgewater's Bryn Mawr Avenue, home to some of the most stylish Victorian era and Jazz Age residential buildings in the city, will take a likely step toward landmark status Thursday.</p><p>The city's Department of Planning and Development will ask the Commission on Chicago Landmarks to approve a preliminary designation to create the Bryn Mawr Avenue Landmark District.</p><p>The proposed three-block, 15-building district between Sheridan Road and Broadway would include some already individually-landmarked structures, such as the eight-story cream and green Belle Shore Apartments, the Tudor-styled Manor House and the elegant princess painted in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2025/10/28/edgewater-beach-apartments-landmark-pink-paint" >“sunset pink,”</a> the Edgewater Beach Apartments, at 5555 N. Sheridan Road.</p><p>"Constructed between 1897 and 1929, the proposed district's 15 contributing buildings form a distinctive and remarkably intact group designed in a range of architectural styles," city staffers wrote in their report to the commission.</p><p>While Bryn Mawr Avenue has been listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1995, a city designation would help protect the corridor's buildings from demolition and unsympathetic alterations. Well, except for 5614 N. Winthrop Ave. and 1106 W. Bryn Mawr Ave. More on that later.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-080000" name="image-080000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ab9d8e1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6541x3671+0+401/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2F4b%2Fff80b1384a6b82cbaee137092c85%2Fbryn-260507-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4c9fbaa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6541x3671+0+401/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2F4b%2Fff80b1384a6b82cbaee137092c85%2Fbryn-260507-19.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d74818/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6541x3671+0+401/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2F4b%2Fff80b1384a6b82cbaee137092c85%2Fbryn-260507-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A portion of the Edgewater Beach Apartments, in "sunset pink," behind the Manor House (right) at 1021-1031 W. Bryn Mawr Ave. in Edgewater." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d74818/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6541x3671+0+401/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2F4b%2Fff80b1384a6b82cbaee137092c85%2Fbryn-260507-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/013206e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6541x3671+0+401/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2F4b%2Fff80b1384a6b82cbaee137092c85%2Fbryn-260507-19.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d74818/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6541x3671+0+401/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2F4b%2Fff80b1384a6b82cbaee137092c85%2Fbryn-260507-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A portion of the “sunset pink” Edgewater Beach Apartments are seen beyond the Manor House (right) at 1021-1031 W. Bryn Mawr Ave. in Edgewater.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>A 15-minute city</h3><p>A century after its creation, the thoroughfare is still a nice little strip with retail space at the bases of many of the street's residential buildings, with the Bryn Mawr stop on the CTA's Red Line right there.</p><p>There's a lot of talk lately about the 15-minute city, a recent urban planning idea that would put everything a resident would need — work, medical care, shopping and the like — within a quarter-hour walk, or transit journey, from home.</p><p>Bryn Mawr Avenue — dense, walkable — had the beginnings of that back when people were barely out of horse-and-buggies. So did spots like South Chicago's Commercial Avenue on the Southeast Side, Six Corners in Portage Park and the Madison and Pulaski retail node in West Garfield Park. The more the city, business and philanthropic community can do to lift these places up, the better.</p><p>"From its earliest period of development, Bryn Mawr Avenue has served as a neighborhood commercial corridor, a street where residents of the surrounding community could access shopping and professional services in a walkable setting close to home," the city's report said.</p><p>Bryn Mawr fell on hard times with vacancies and crumbing buildings in the 1970s and 1980s, "but the street had a bit of a renaissance 30 years ago," Edgewater Historical Society President John Holden said.</p><p>"It went from pretty bad to pretty good, but then the last seven to 10 years have been kind of rough between COVID and the reconstruction of the Red Line," he said. "So there's really been a lot of focus, especially amongst the people who live right along the street, to try to get the street back to its former glory."</p><p>To help this along, the city in 2025 created the Broadway Land Use Framework, which seeks to improve the intersection of Bryn Mawr and Broadway and "celebrate the unique history and culture of the corridor," according to the plan.</p><p>And a permanent landmark designation for Bryn Mawr, if approved by the City Council, would make buildings within the district eligible for property tax incentives that could help write-down renovation and restoration costs.</p><p>But not for 5614 N. Winthrop and 1106 W. Bryn Mawr. The city staffers' report said the two buildings have been altered so much that it recommends the commission approve demo permits for the structures.</p><h3>Extra, extra!</h3><p>The landmarks commission will also vote on whether to recommend the City Council approve a permanent landmark designation for Holabird & Root's former Chicago Daily News Building, 400 W. Madison St.</p><p>This mighty fine 26-story riverside slab of Art Deco — with its beautifully chiseled and sculpted exteriors and fine 1920s bottom floor spaces — represents the best of Chicago architecture and should have been landmarked decades ago.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fe0000" name="image-fe0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d1980e9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4228x2373+0+622/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2F5b%2F20638e5e4f3c9bf75b94aa3a9f0f%2Fdsc-1021.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b9a403b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4228x2373+0+622/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2F5b%2F20638e5e4f3c9bf75b94aa3a9f0f%2Fdsc-1021.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae53a9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4228x2373+0+622/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2F5b%2F20638e5e4f3c9bf75b94aa3a9f0f%2Fdsc-1021.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Exterior of the former Chicago Daily News Building at 2 N. Riverside Plaza." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae53a9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4228x2373+0+622/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2F5b%2F20638e5e4f3c9bf75b94aa3a9f0f%2Fdsc-1021.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1413fe2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4228x2373+0+622/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2F5b%2F20638e5e4f3c9bf75b94aa3a9f0f%2Fdsc-1021.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ae53a9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4228x2373+0+622/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcc%2F5b%2F20638e5e4f3c9bf75b94aa3a9f0f%2Fdsc-1021.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The former Chicago Daily News Building, 2 N. Riverside Plaza.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Lee Bey/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But the structure's long-time and clout-heavy former owner, Sam Zell, forbade it.</p><p>Zell <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2023/5/18/23728642/sam-zell-dies-obituary-billionaire-chicago-equity-group" >died in 2023</a> and the building’s new owner, 2 N. Riverside Venture, is embracing the landmark designation. The group plans a $70 million renovation that would preserve the structure's historic exteriors, riverfront plaza, first- and second-floor lobby spaces and that 200-foot-long concourse that leads to the Ogilvie Transportation Center pedestrian bridge.</p><p>And what about the kinetic-looking, 180-foot John Warner Norton mural that once adorned the concourse's concave ceiling?</p><p>The work, titled "Gathering the News, Printing the News, Transporting the News," went into hiding after Zell snatched it down decades ago — but the new owners have deed to the masterpiece.</p><p>A permanent landmark designation would make the developers eligible for a $29 million real estate tax break, spread out across 12 years.</p><p>With that kind of taxpayer cash at play, the Daily News Building's owners should do the public a solid in return: Restore the mural and put it back in the concourse — where it belongs.</p>
entries.author: Lee Bey

entries.title: El Centro Presidencial Obama agota entradas en su primer mes
entries.summary: <p>Solo horas después de <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/08/venta-de-boletos-para-el-museo-del-centro-presidencial-obama-comienza-el-6-de-mayo" target="_blank" >salir a la venta</a> el miércoles por la mañana, las entradas para el Centro Presidencial Obama se agotaron para el primer mes, con más entradas “volando” de las estanterías digitales a medida que avanzaba el día.</p><p>Andrés Chamorro, residente de Lake View y originario de Nueva Jersey, dijo que aún no era tan malo como intentar conseguir entradas para shows locales de grandes artistas.</p><p>“Diría que esto fue menos estresante que tratar de conseguir entradas para [la banda de rock] Geese”, dijo Chamorro. “Fue doloroso perderme el fin de semana inaugural, pero me enfrentaba a buena parte de la ciudad, si no es que el estado”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-0d0000" name="module-0d0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El Centro Presidencial Obama no respondió de inmediato a una solicitud de comentario ni proporcionó cifras sobre cuántas entradas se vendieron el miércoles.</p><p>El museo debutará el 18 de junio con una ceremonia de apertura solo por invitación y abrirá al público al día siguiente, el día de Juneteenth. El mes pasado, los donantes tuvieron acceso anticipado a las entradas, por lo que quienes buscaban entrar el día de la inauguración ya enfrentaban una batalla difícil desde el principio.</p><p>Chamorro tenía la intención de conseguir entradas para el fin de semana inaugural para él y su novio, pero ya se habían agotado cuando logró avanzar en la fila.</p><p>“He visto cómo la zona ha cambiado y se ha revitalizado a lo largo de los años, y hemos visto cómo levantaron el obelisco”, agregó Chamorro. “Así que ver la culminación el día de la apertura hubiera sido genial”.</p><p>Todo el mes de junio y la mitad de julio ya se han agotado, aunque el calendario en gran parte sigue abierto hasta noviembre. Octubre y noviembre incluye todos los martes, que son días gratuitos para los residentes de Illinois.</p><p>Las entradas cuestan $30, aunque hay un descuento de $4 para los residentes de Illinois, un descuento de $3 para niños de fuera del estado y $11 de descuento para niños de dentro del estado. Las entradas no pueden ser devueltas, transferidas ni vendidas.</p><p>Chamorro planea conseguir entradas eventualmente y sugiere a otros que intenten conseguirlas para un día entre semana, para hacer todo el proceso más fácil, incluso si la primera ola de ventas parece haber terminado. Agregó que podría intentar evitar los fines de semana o momentos en que las escuelas probablemente lleven a estudiantes.</p><p>“Para algo como esto, es algo que quiero experimentar del todo”, añadió.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Authorities excavate at South Elgin home looking for missing teen
entries.summary: <p>Kane County authorities are performing a “forensic excavation” at a South Elgin home in an attempt to find a teenage girl who has been missing for a decade.</p><p>Kane County State’s Attorney Jamie Mosser said ground penetrating radar scans at the residence on the 800 block of Revere Road revealed “anomalies” beneath the surface the detectives believe warranted further examination.</p><p>A portion of Revere Road is closed off.</p><p>South Elgin police and the Fox Valley Major Crimes Task Force are conducting the search.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260507/crime/authorities-excavate-near-south-elgin-home-looking-for-missing-teen/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>.<br></p>

enforcement continues</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-d/" target="_blank" >Adriana Cardona-Maguigad</a></h5><p><b>ICE not out</b>: Immigration enforcement hasn’t ended. Five hundred and eighty people have been detained in the Chicago area from Jan.1 through mid-March, according to a WBEZ/Sun-Times analysis of data from the Deportation Data Project, a collective of lawyers and academics. Organizers and other rapid responders who try to warn people about enforcement activity say they remain on high alert and are shifting tactics.</p><p><b>The tactics</b>: Organizers say they are planning training sessions and deploying volunteer responders to verify immigration enforcement when they receive an alert — and aim to be better prepared if large numbers of agents return to Chicago.</p><p><b>Shifted strategies</b>: Building on lessons learned from last year in Chicago and Minneapolis, shifted strategies include tapping new dispatch systems to speed responses when agents are spotted and encouraging block-by-block, hyperlocal communication. Some groups are training people to prepare for more aggressive enforcement tactics and to deal with obstacles immigrants might face if detained.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-h/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MORE IN IMMIGRATION ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c70000" name="image-c70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d58f0db/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Fb6%2F5f7d845b4a888b9764faaa9e419e%2Fjo7a3223.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/79813ec/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Fb6%2F5f7d845b4a888b9764faaa9e419e%2Fjo7a3223.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/51b33eb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Fb6%2F5f7d845b4a888b9764faaa9e419e%2Fjo7a3223.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A demonstrator who was pushed to the ground by ICE agents is helped up by another protester Saturday in Broadview. She stood naked and handcuffed for nearly 10 minutes before officers allowed her to get dressed as they carried out a search warrant on what turned out to be the wrong home. Since then, Young has pushed for state- and city-level bans on no-knock warrants, so far to no avail — though there have been changes made to Chicago police's search warrant policy.</p><p><a Friday at St. Andrew’s Greek Orthodox Church in Edgewater. Officials will close traffic in anticipation of the many mourners expected.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-e/" target="_blank" ><b>Peoples Gas seeks hike</b></a>: The Illinois attorney general’s office and consumer advocates say Peoples Gas' proposal to raise rates by $202 million would ultimately cost homeowners an average $130 per year while lining shareholders' pockets.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-s/" target="_blank" ><b>Brookfield Zoo strike ends</b></a>: The strike ended on its third day after zoo and union officials reached a tentative four-year collective bargaining agreement. The deal covers staff members in animal care, facilities, custodial and security roles, the zoo said.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-g/" target="_blank" ><b>Pope’s bank hung up</b></a>: The Rev. Tom McCarthy recently shared a story about how, two months into his pontificate, Pope Leo XIV called his bank in South Chicago to change his phone number and address. The teller hung up on him.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-w/" target="_blank" ><b>New beach parking gates</b></a>: The Chicago Park District installed automated parking gates at 10 lots on the city’s lakefront and is using license plate readers to track when drivers enter and exit.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-yd/" target="_blank" ><b>Millennium Park music</b></a>: Running June 15-Aug. 6, the Summer Music Series will host free performances from Sheila E., Arrested Development, DJ Ca$h Era and many other artists.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-yh/" target="_blank" ><b>‘The Bear’ takes a bow</b></a>: June 25 is the premiere date of the Chicago-set show's fifth and final season.</li></ul><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻 </h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-jl/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-beb20633-4a16-11f1-83ca-1de02daf3060"><li><b>Midway Blitz doc</b>: A new short film revisits the tense, illegal arrest of a man in Albany Park — and the community's resistance. Maria Inés Zamudio of Invisible Institute and Bill Morrison, the film's director, will discuss.<br></li><li><b>Rohingya history</b>: Abdul Jabbar Amanullah of the Rohingya Culture Center talks about his journey fleeing Malaysia for America and the history of Chicago's Rohingya community.<br></li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-jr/" target="_blank" ><b>Say More with Mary Dixon and Patrick Smith, 10 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-beb20636-4a16-11f1-83ca-1de02daf3060"><li><b>Cost coping</b>: The price of food, fuel and other necessities has skyrocketed. How are Chicagoans making ends meet? Callers weigh in.<br></li><li><b>Mother Goose reimagined</b>: Musicians Natalie Merchant and Matt Gold stop by to discuss Cabinet of Wonder, a multimedia work that reimagines Mother Goose and her famous canon of rhymes for a new generation.<br></li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-jy/" target="_blank" ><b>LISTEN LIVE 🎧</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX ⚖️🏀⚾</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-beb22d41-4a16-11f1-83ca-1de02daf3060"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-jj/" target="_blank" ><b>CTU threatens suit</b></a>: The Chicago Teachers Union is threatening to file a lawsuit against Chicago Public Schools over the alleged delayed payment of some $300,000 in stipends for high school athletic directors.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>Introducing Bryson Graham</b></a>: Bulls team president Michael Reinsdorf introduced Graham, the new executive vice president of basketball operations, in a press conference that started with an apology and ended with promise.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-ji/" target="_blank" ><b>Ajša Sivka heads to college</b></a>: Sivka, a first-round draft pick for the Sky, was expected to join the team this season. Instead, she has signed with the University of Kentucky.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-jd/" target="_blank" ><b>Here for the vibes</b></a>: class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Looks from Monday’s Chicago Does the Met Gala.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-tl/" target="_blank" >Chicago hosts its own Met Gala</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-tr/" target="_blank" >Brittany Sowacke</a></h5><p>While celebrities turned up Monday at New York City's annual Met Gala, local fashion fans gathered at an events venue in Streeterville for the fifth annual Chicago Does the Met Gala, thrown by the Chicago Fashion Coalition.</p><p>There were high fashion hijabs, towering hair pieces, stilettos and chunky boots. Ball gowns and feather collars, both handmade and bespoke.</p><p>New York's theme was "fashion is art," which the Chicago bash remixed, telling attendees to take an artwork by a local artist and translate that into their look. </p><p>More than 300 people showed up to the fashion celebration at Chez Event Venue. Some stuck to the assignment, donning hand-painted garments. Others just wanted to put on something and have a good time, like one attendee who wore a banana costume.</p><p>"We don’t get to choose our bodies, but we get to choose how we decorate them," Fashion Coalition President Marquan Jones said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-ty/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Mother's Day is Sunday, so we want to know: <b>What's something valuable you learned from your mom?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Respond to this newsletter</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wditkdd-l-tt/" target="_blank" >After companies charged consumers higher prices on items due to the Trump administration’s tariffs, what do you think of companies getting refunds from the government?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said, edited for space and clarity:</p><p>"This money was paid by consumers and should be returned to them, not corporations. They already have our money." — <i>Christine Bock</i></p><p>"That money belongs to the consumers. We have felt the brunt of extremely skyrocketing prices and have suffered since this administration took office, and companies have made astronomical revenue off of our backs." — <i>Patrice Brownlee Lester</i></p><p>"The companies passed on all or a big part of the tariff taxes to us, the customers; now they get a refund, but we do not. So the company gets paid twice, and we get taxed twice." — <i>Kirk Melhuish</i></p><p>"This is a huge rip-off to small businesses and consumers. Only big companies, the direct importers on file, will get refunds ... They are NOT refunding this reimbursement to their wholesale or retail customers. This 'reimbursement' just worked out as a huge windfall for these importers ... I prefer the government keep the money and instead pay down debt." — <i>Stacy McCaskill</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>PICTURE CHICAGO 📸</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div entries.title: The Sky built a deeper roster, and Tyler Marsh isn’t worried about its quirks
entries.summary: <p>When Tyler Marsh was introduced as head coach of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> in November of 2024, he shared a lesson from Las Vegas, where he won two championships as an assistant. To be successful in the W, you can't just rely on your stars. You need others to get hot at the right time. Think Aces guard Riquna Williams’ 17 points in Game 4 of the 2022 Finals.</p><p>Marsh wanted to build a team where players one through 12 could make an impact.</p><p>This 2026 Sky roster is a solid attempt.</p><p>You could employ a starting five of Skylar Diggins, Natasha Cloud, Rickea Jackson, Azurá Stevens and Kamilla Cardoso — and still bring an all-time great in Courtney Vandersloot, plus two <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/05/03/sky-defense-needs-dijonai-carrington-back-and-soon" >All-Defensive talents in DiJonai Carrington</a> and Elizabeth Williams off the bench.</p><p>You’d also have your <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/21/whats-not-to-love-about-sky-rookie-gabriela-jaquez" >2026 lottery pick, Gabriela Jaquez</a>, and your best three-point shooter, Rachel Banham, to mix in.</p><p>That’s depth to be proud of. It’s just that the composition is a little uneven.</p><p>For starters, the balance between bigs and littles is skewed. The Sky have eight guards and four post players—and that's if you count Jackson (6’2) as a true post.</p><p>Won’t that leave them vulnerable on the interior?</p><p>"I don't think you can go wrong with a lot of good guards, capable guards," Marsh said Tuesday after practice. "It's not like we're just stock holding a bunch of players. It's players that can contribute and we're going to find ways to utilize them."</p><p>Marsh isn’t short on ideas for how to make it work. Expect plenty of three-guard lineups. Expect the team to prioritize transition defense over offensive rebounding. Expect Cloud, a versatile defender, to match up on post players.</p><p>But what about the tension between Marsh's offensive system, which relies on 3-point shooting, and the personnel?</p><p>The Sky added guards who can create shots and get to the rack, but only a few are known for spotting up and knocking down treys.</p><p>"I think we have some underrated shooters," Marsh said. "We have players that you may not classify as shooters but have the ability to hit shots in big moments."</p><p>Marsh emphasized that Diggins shot 36.5% from deep last season, almost four points above her career average. He also pointed out that Cloud lit the Sky up from deep in their home opener last season.</p><p>"We got some players that we trust that can make open shots," Marsh said. "It's about creating for them and putting them in position to feel confident to take and make those shots."</p><p>Ultimately this is the outlook you want from your coach. You want them to see strengths in their players where others don’t. You want them to trust their own creativity and schemes to protect the team’s vulnerabilities.</p><p>Still — doesn’t <i>anything</i> concern the second-year coach ahead of the regular season opener on Saturday? What are the top two worries keeping him up at night?</p><p>"Staying healthy, and staying healthy," Marsh said Wednesday after practice.</p><p>Which is to be expected. Injuries derailed the Sky last year, and they’re off on the wrong foot again this season. Carrington and Vandersloot are both recovering from surgery and are weeks away from returning. And Stevens is still ramping back up from a knee injury at Unrivaled.</p><p>Funny how three injuries to key players throws cold water on that depth pretty fast.</p><p>Suddenly, the starting five might be Diggins, Cloud, Banham, Jackson, and Cardoso, with Williams and Jacy Sheldon first up off the bench. Not quite the same punch to it, but for now it will have to suffice. </p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Man, 18, allegedly fled after fatally gunning down father in Oak Lawn, police say
entries.summary: <p>An 18-year-old man is facing charges he gunned down his father earlier this week in southwest suburban Oak Lawn. </p><p>The deadly shooting happened Monday in the 10400 block of South Austin Avenue, where patrol officers responded about 10:45 p.m. for a report of a person shot. Inside a home there police found Ayed Mdanat, 50, suffering from gunshot wounds, police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. </p><p>Mdanat was taken to Advocate Christ Medical Center in Oak Lawn, where he was pronounced dead less than an hour later, at 11:18 p.m., according to the medical examiner's office. An autopsy Tuesday determined he died of multiple gunshot wounds and his death was ruled a homicide, according to the medical examiner’s office.</p><p>A large-caliber firearm was recovered at the scene.</p><p>Responding officers quickly identified the offender as the victim’s son, 18-year-old Sami<br>Mdanat, who had fled the area on foot. Officers established an outer perimeter, and the 18-year-old Mdanat was located a short time later near the scene, police said. </p><p>Detectives executed several search warrants, which resulted in murder charges for Mdanat, who was scheduled for a detention hearing in Bridgeview. </p><p>Details from the hearing were not immediately available.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Teens arrested, but Arlington Heights school resource officer’s gun still missing
entries.summary: <p>Two teens face felony charges in connection with the disappearance of an Arlington Heights police officer’s gun after it was mistakenly left in a school bathroom Monday, authorities announced Wednesday.</p><p>However, the whereabouts of the officer’s gun remain unknown and recovering it remains “our highest priority,” police said.</p><p>The charges follow an investigation launched Monday when a school resource officer at Forest View Educational Center in Arlington Heights reported his service weapon missing, officials said. The officer reported that he had removed the firearm from his holster before using the bathroom, authorities said.</p><p>Authorities now allege that Juan Pablo Sanchez Jaramillo, an 18-year-old student from Mount Prospect, took the unattended firearm from the restroom after school was dismissed for the day.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260506/crime/teens-arrested-but-arlington-heights-school-resource-officers-gun-still-missing/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>. <br></p>
entries.author: Charles Keeshan | Daily Herald

entries.title: Distrito de Parques de Chicago instala rejas de estacionamiento automatizadas en 10 playas
entries.summary: <p>Las personas que visiten las playas de Chicago pueden notar cambios en varios estacionamientos en la costa del lago de la ciudad este verano, ya que la Municipalidad implementa un nuevo sistema de gestión de pagos "modernizado".</p><p>El Distrito de Parques de Chicago instaló rejas de estacionamiento automatizadas en 10 lotes en la costa del lago de la ciudad y utiliza lectores de placas para registrar cuándo los conductores entran y salen.</p><p>Sin embargo, el pago en los lotes no es algo nuevo. El Distrito de Parques dice que los cambios recientes "simplemente modernizan la forma en que se gestionan y aplican los pagos". El distrito también mencionó que las rejas puertas ayudarán con la seguridad en los parques.</p><p>Los lotes, administrados por SP+, han sido estacionamientos de pago desde 2009.</p><p>Bajo el sistema, se permitirá a los visitantes un periodo de gracia gratuito de 15 minutos sin cargo que se puede utilizar para recoger, dejar o descargar suministros. Después de eso, se aplicará una tarifa de estacionamiento. Las tarifas de estacionamiento no han aumentado como resultado de las mejoras, según el Distrito de Parques.</p><p>Las tarifas de estacionamiento varían ligeramente según la ubicación, con tarifas de alrededor de $4.07 por hasta una hora y un máximo de $24 para vehículos estacionados por más de nueve horas.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> entries.title: Sindicato de Maestros amenaza con demandar a CPS por no pagarles a docenas de directores deportivos
entries.summary: <p>El Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago (CTU) está amenazando con presentar una demanda contra las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) por lo que los abogados del sindicato dicen que es el retraso en el pago de unos $300,000 en estipendios para directores atléticos de escuelas secundarias.</p><p>El lunes, un abogado que representa al sindicato envió una carta al distrito diciendo que “para evitar una demanda” CPS debería distribuir los estipendios de $7,525 a los directores atléticos de 41 escuelas secundarias que no han sido pagados por su trabajo durante la temporada de deportes de otoño. El abogado del sindicato dio al distrito un plazo hasta el jueves.</p><p>La ley de Illinois requiere que los sueldos se paguen a más tardar 13 días después del período de pago correspondiente, y “ahora estamos meses pasados de esa fecha límite”, escribió el abogado Josiah Groff. La temporada de deportes de otoño por lo general concluye en diciembre. Antes de este año escolar, escribió Groff, los directores atléticos típicamente recibían su pago dos meses después de que terminaba su temporada.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e30000" name="module-e30000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Según la carta, el distrito tampoco ha pagado estipendios a casi 70 directores atléticos correspondientes de la temporada de invierno, que concluyó a finales de marzo. El sindicato dijo que tomaría acciones legales si esos pagos no se realizan pronto.</p><p>Un portavoz de CPS dijo que el distrito está revisando las afirmaciones hechas en la carta y que aproximadamente la mitad de los directores atléticos mencionados tienen programado recibir su pago el 15 de mayo, aunque no estaba claro para qué temporada sería.</p><p>“Los otros ya han recibido el pago o no cumplen con los requisitos de elegibilidad para el estipendio en este momento”, dijo el portavoz en un comunicado. “El distrito está enfocado en procesar estos pagos y está abordando el problema a través de los canales laborales apropiados”.</p><p>El distrito ha estado lidiando con problemas financieros continuos. Se proyecta que CPS terminará el año escolar con un déficit de $45 millones y que podría enfrentar un déficit de $529 millones el próximo año. Recientemente, CPS se vio envuelto en un desacuerdo con la Arquidiócesis de Chicago sobre si el distrito estaba reteniendo fondos federales destinados a estudiantes con discapacidades que asisten a escuelas privadas.</p><p>En la carta, Groff atribuyó el retraso en los estipendios a un “desacuerdo interno dentro de CPS” sobre si el pago debería provenir del presupuesto central del distrito o de los presupuestos individuales de las escuelas.</p><p>Wendy Weingarten, la enlace del comité deportivo de CTU, dijo que cada director atlético debería recibir el estipendio y que esto debería provenir del presupuesto central del distrito. Weingarten y algunos directores atléticos afirman que los funcionarios de CPS le han dicho recientemente al sindicato que los directores atléticos de grandes secundarias no son elegibles para el estipendio.</p><p>Según el abogado del CTU, las 41 escuelas secundarias en las que los directores atléticos no han recibido pago son algunas de las más grandes del distrito, entre ellas Lane Tech, Whitney Young, Amundsen, Jones y Curie.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.ctulocal1.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/CBA-24-2026-03-20.pdf" target="_blank" >Según el último acuerdo</a> entre CPS y CTU, el distrito es responsable de financiar a un director atlético de tiempo completo para las secundarias grandes y extra grandes, además de reservar al menos $3 millones por año para usar en estipendios para directores atléticos a medio tiempo y enlaces atléticos de escuelas primarias, que actúan como directores atléticos para los grados menores.</p><p>“Si la junta está proporcionando esos $3 millones, no sé por qué las escuelas pequeñas y medianas tienen que usar sus propios presupuestos para pagar”, agregó Weingarten.</p><p>El contrato también establece que los estipendios de $7,525 para directores atléticos están destinados a escuelas secundarias sin un puesto de director atlético de tiempo completo.</p><p>Emily Crawford, quien es la directora atlética de tiempo completo en la Secundaria Chicago Academy, es una de las que no ha recibido su estipendio por la temporada de otoño. Es responsable de muchas tareas, como programar autobuses para los juegos, coordinar pagos para torneos, uniformes y equipos, y rastrear la elegibilidad de los atletas.</p><p>Crawford no tiene asistente, por lo que es responsable de coordinar a los 300 atletas de la escuela por sí sola. “Estamos en el extremo más pequeño de las grandes”, dijo Crawford. El estipendio ayuda a compensar las horas extra que dedica, dijo.</p><p>“Creo que la semana pasada no salí de la escuela durante varios días hasta las 8 p.m.", comentó Crawford.</p><p>Piensa que todos los directores atléticos deberían recibir un estipendio y que la idea de que solo las escuelas más pequeñas lo reciban es retrógrada.</p><p>“Las escuelas más grandes están haciendo sustancialmente más trabajo que una escuela que sólo tiene ocho programas atléticos. Tenemos más estudiantes, más atletas, más entrenadores, más personal haciendo más trabajo”, agregó Crawford. “Creo que es muy desalentador y desvalorizante que el trabajo no esté siendo reconocido”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Sky bring back Maddy Westbeld and Aicha Coulibaly on developmental contracts
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> are bringing back Maddy Westbeld and Aicha Coulibaly on development contracts. Both players <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/05/04/sky-waive-maddy-westbeld-aicha-coulibaly" >had been waived earlier this week.</a></p><p>Development contracts are new to the WNBA this year. In addition to the 12 roster spots, two development players can practice with the team and play in up to 12 games. They receive a weekly stipend and pro-rated salary for games played. </p><p>If the Sky roster gets depleted by injury, Westbeld and Coulibaly could be signed to a full spot. Last season a wave of injuries hit and the Sky had to sign Marquesha Davis to a hardship contract and Sevgi Uzun to a rest-of-season contract. Pulling up Westbeld and Coulibaly would be smoother.</p><p>However, other teams in the league can also offer development players a full spot at any time. The Sky would have right-of-first-refusal to offer a full spot on the roster, otherwise the player could take the other deal. </p><p>The Sky selected Westbeld No. 16 in the 2025 draft while Coulibaly was their No. 22 pick in the 2025 draft. Westbeld averaged 13.6 minutes per game in her rookie season and scored 4.1 points per game. Coulibaly did not join the team because she was rehabbing an ACL injury. </p><p></p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Cubs reliever Ben Brown could replace Matthew Boyd in rotation
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> manager Craig Counsell said right-hander Ben Brown, who started 15 games for last season and has been the Cubs’ most reliable reliever this season, could join the starting rotation in the absence of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/05/06/cubs-matthew-boyd-surgery-knee-meniscus-craig-counsell-wrigley-field" >injured left-hander Matthew Boyd.</a></p><p>Boyd underwent surgery to remove a torn part of the meniscus cartilage in his left knee Thursday morning, the club announced. The procedure, called an arthroscopic partial meniscectomy, is designed to ease pain and locking in the knee. Boyd is expected to be on crutches for a few days before beginning rehabilitation.</p><p></p><p>“We know he’s going to miss six weeks,’’ Counsell said. “That’s what we’re hoping for. Obviously, we’ll see how it goes. I think the important thing is, how much time he misses throwing. The knee’s gonna recover quickly, but how much throwing down time do we have.’’</p><p>Asked about Brown taking Boyd’s place in the rotation, Counsell said: “I think just covering the innings is kind of what we’re thinking about. So yeah, that’s one of the things we’ve talked about, absolutely.’’</p><p>Brown said he would be open to starting.</p><p>“Yeah, whatever it is, just put my best foot forward,’’ he said. “I like where I’m at. So whatever it is.’’</p><p>Brown said he didn’t think it would take long for him to be stretched out as a starter. Counsell said he has made a point of giving Brown multiple innings. He went 3 1/3 innings in each of his first two appearances this season, and has gone at least two innings in eight of his next 10 appearances. He has thrown a team-high 25 2/3 innings out of the pen, posting a 2.10 ERA while striking out 10 and walking eight. Four of those walks came in his first three appearances.</p><p>Brown began last season in the rotation, but was dropped after a rough outing in St. Louis on June 23, when he gave up eight runs on nine hits in five innings, including four home runs. He made one start thereafter, on July 27, when he allowed just three hits and a run in a 5-4 win over the White Sox.</p><h3>Roster moves</h3><p>Corbin Martin, who inherited a 4-2 lead in the ninth inning Wednesday and gave up three runs without retiring a batter, was designated for assignment Thursday morning. To take his place on the roster, the Cubs promoted right-hander Gavin Hollowell from Iowa. He had 15 strikeouts and a 2.25 ERA in eight innings in Triple-A.</p><p>Over Martin’s last three outings, he had allowed eight base runners (four hits, four walks) and six earned runs while retiring just three batters.</p><p>Martin, whose career has been derailed by Tommy John surgery and an additional arm injury in which the tendon on his humerus was pulled violently off the bone and had to be surgically reattached, had signed a minor-league deal with the Cubs in January. He had one save for the Cubs.</p><h3>Mr. Walkoff</h3><h3></h3><p>With his bases-loaded walk in the 10th inning of Wednesday night’s 7-6 win over the Reds, Michael Busch became the fourth player in franchise history to be credited with a walkoff RBI in consecutive games. Busch singled home ghost runner Dansby Swanson with the winning run in the 10th inning of Tuesday night’s 3-2 win over the Reds.</p><p>Starlin Castro (2015), Ron Santo (1966) and Bob Will (1961) are the other Cubs to have accomplished the feat. Santo did it most dramatically with two extra-inning home runs: a 12th-inning, three-run homer off Ted Abernathy of the Braves on May 28, 1966, then a 10th-inning solo home run off Billy O’Dell the next afternoon. </p><p>Abernathy began the day in the home team’s clubhouse, but just before noon was traded to the Braves, and entered the game for his new team in the eighth. </p><p>Santo’s walkoff the next day came after Eddie Mathews of the Braves tied the score with a controversial inside-the-park home run in the ninth inning that led to the ejection of Cubs manager Leo Durocher.</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Chicago Sinfonietta to pause programs until 2027 and lay off staff amid financial challenges
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Sinfonietta will pause artistic and educational activities at the end of its 2025-26 season this month amid financial challenges, the organization <a class="Link" href="https://chicagosinfonietta.org/strategic-renewal-period/" target="_blank" ><u>announced Thursday</u></a>.</p><p>A spokesperson confirmed that the group will lay off its seven administrative staffers, leaving President and CEO Sidney Jackson as the only full-time employee. The orchestra will still play this weekend’s concerts, including a rendition of Gershwin’s “Rhapsody in Blue,” a tribute to Miles Davis and a performance of William Dawson’s “Negro Folk Symphony,” in Naperville and Evanston.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ca0000" name="image-ca0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f35dd0e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fcf%2F21b92859401b804d644c67bfb806%2Fbondsmusic-2602270177.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/093762d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fcf%2F21b92859401b804d644c67bfb806%2Fbondsmusic-2602270177.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/440733c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fcf%2F21b92859401b804d644c67bfb806%2Fbondsmusic-2602270177.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Sidney Jackson Jr., President and CEO of Chicago Sinfonietta poses for a portrait along the Chicago River in downtown Chicago on February 26, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/440733c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fcf%2F21b92859401b804d644c67bfb806%2Fbondsmusic-2602270177.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/94be2bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fcf%2F21b92859401b804d644c67bfb806%2Fbondsmusic-2602270177.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/440733c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fcf%2F21b92859401b804d644c67bfb806%2Fbondsmusic-2602270177.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sidney Jackson Jr., President and CEO of Chicago Sinfonietta poses for a portrait along the Chicago River in downtown Chicago on February 26, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The orchestra plans to relaunch public programs in 2027, which marks its 40th anniversary. It will also present a fundraising event tied to MLK Day. </p><p>Concert attendance and audience contributions have not returned to pre-COVID-19 pandemic levels, according to a statement. As a result, the organization will focus on fundraising and sustainability planning during a “strategic renewal period.” Patrons can visit <a class="Link" href="http://chicagosinfonietta.org/give" target="_blank" ><u>chicagosinfonietta.org/give</u></a> to make donations, which will be matched up to $25,000 by an anonymous donor.</p><p>“While we have made significant mission-driven and programmatic progress in recent years, we cannot be sustainable if we don’t make changes to our operating model,” said Chicago Sinfonietta CEO Sidney Jackson in a release. “This strategic pause will allow us to reimagine future artistic and educational programs that will also be financially positive. Future seasons and programming may look different but will be rooted both in our mission and a growth mindset, operating from a position of strength and sustainability.”</p><div class="Enhancement" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bbd1dd5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F41%2F7fa88b36451f903165296ee99447%2Fcoffeehouse-080621-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2213018/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F41%2F7fa88b36451f903165296ee99447%2Fcoffeehouse-080621-05.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bbd1dd5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F94%2F41%2F7fa88b36451f903165296ee99447%2Fcoffeehouse-080621-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Janice MacDonald, left, and Dileep Gangolli, right, of the Chicago Sinfonietta play outside at the Back of the Yards Coffeehouse at 2059 W 47th St in Back of the Yards, Thursday, Aug. 5, 2021. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Paul Freeman founded the Chicago Sinfionetta in 1987 with a mission to advance equity in classical music by developing diverse talent and reaching underserved audiences. The organization’s pause in programming is happening during a precarious time for the arts sector. In the last year, President Donald Trump has <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/03/restoring-truth-and-sanity-to-american-history/" target="_blank" ><u>criticized the Smithsonian</u></a> for “divisive, race-centered ideology” and made <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2025/04/04/trump-humanities-grants-funding-cuts-chicago-illinois-culture-national-endowment" target="_blank" ><u>cuts to federal arts funding</u></a> for organizations that fail to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2025/02/14/trumps-new-arts-funding-guidelines-chicago-nea-arts-grants-jeopardy" target="_blank" ><u>prohibit programs supporting diversity, equity and inclusion</u></a>.</p><p>“Securing the future of this organization is crucial,” Music Director Mei-Ann Chen said in a statement. “Being entrusted with its musical identity by Maestro Freeman himself and the board 15 years ago is an artistic responsibility I treasure, especially for the unique mission championing DEI through innovative programming. I am proud to stand by and honored to work with Sinfonietta’s musicians — without their shared passion and talent, Maestro Freeman’s vision would not exist today.”<br></p>
entries.author: Erica Thompson

entries.title: Bears waive CB Zah Frazier, a Round 5 pick just a year ago
entries.summary: <p>Cornerback <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/04/26/bears-nfl-draft-2025-utsa-zah-frazier-round-5-cornerback-texas-san-antonio" target="_blank" >Zah Frazier</a>, a fifth-round pick from 2025 who sat out all last season with what the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> called a personal issue, was waived on Thursday.</p><p>The Bears were intrigued by the UTSA cornerback’s 6-3 frame — gigantic, for a cornerback — but learned of the personal issue after the draft. Frazier was put on the non-football injury/illness list in August, costing him his rookie season.</p><p>In January, general manager Ryan Poles said that Frazier had a “mountain to climb” to contribute in 2026 because he missed so much time last season.</p><p>“He needed to play,” Poles said.</p><p>Had Frazier remained on the roster, he would have been eligible to participate in the team’s rookie minicamp, which starts Friday morning at Halas Hall. The Bears drafted Texas cornerback Malik Muhammad in Round 4 last month — he figured to be ahead of Frazier in the pecking order this year.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: White Sox, Munetaka Murakami in no rush to extend contract
entries.summary: <p>ANAHEIM, Calif. — The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> may have a choice regarding the future in Chicago of Japanese slugger Munetaka Murakami. But then again, they may have no choice at all. He will be a free agent after the 2027 season, unless there is an extended lockout.</p><p>And now the genie is out of the bottle. The other 29 teams that failed to sign him — either interested or not — have now seen what he’s done in his six weeks since joining Major League Baseball on a two-year, $34 million contract. The financial numbers the second time around will dwarf that.</p><p>The Sox should try to extend him right now before it’s too late, although a member of the organization said Wednesday there have been no current talks.</p><p>Murakami said through his interpreter that right now he’s fine with all that.</p><p>“It’s very early in the season and still the start of my contract,” he said. “I’m really happy where I am at the moment. I’m not looking too far ahead, but if that time comes and the White Sox obviously feel happy with me and want to extend me more, I’d consider that an honor and that would be a happy situation for me as well.”</p><p>For the moment, he’s coming home as the Sox open a nine-game stand Friday night at Rate Field against the Mariners, not only having hit 14 homers with a .565 slugging percentage and .934 OPS, but he’s helped recast the team with his leadership and personality.</p><p>From <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/05/02/white-sox-munetaka-murakami-sushi-dinner-san-diego-colson-montgomery-sam-antonucci-sean-burke-mike-vasil-chase-meidroth-noah-schultz-kenzo-yagi-shohei-ohtani" >a team sushi dinner</a> to coming off the bench Tuesday night during a forced off-day to take two at-bats and play first base the rest of the game, Murakami has shown himself as a selfless teammate as interested in turning the previously moribund Sox into a winner as he is in establishing his own big-league presence.</p><p>“Let’s see what happens,” he added. “I’m just focusing on what’s in front of me and trying to get the results as much as possible. I’m not looking too far ahead. I’m trying to concentrate on what I can do at the moment.”</p><p>Murakami had a monster game on Monday in the Sox’s 6-0 win over the Angels, their only victory during the three-game series at Angel Stadium. He went 3-for-4, with a walk, a single, his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/05/05/after-hitting-his-14th-homer-munetaka-murakami-finally-got-his-first-double" >first major-league double</a> and a mammoth two-run homer into the shrubbery beyond the center field fence measuring 429 feet. His homer tied Aaron Judge at the time, but the Yankees star right-fielder has since hit No. 15 to retake the lead.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">MUNETAKA MURAKAMI!<br><br>He ties Aaron Judge for the MLB home run lead with No. 14! 😤 <a href="https://t.co/2flNFsZgbT">pic.twitter.com/2flNFsZgbT</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2051491104332145131?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">May 5, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The homer Monday came in the fourth and the double in the sixth, leading Murakami to muse after that game, “It was going to come sooner or later so I’m really happy the double is out of the way.”</p><p>It’s not as if Murakami hasn’t hit doubles before. He hit 148 in eight seasons for the Yakult Swallows in Nippon Professional Baseball and 173 overall between foreign play and the World Baseball Classic. As far as homers are concerned he hit 246 in Japan and 265 around the rest of the world.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">🚨 MUNETAKA MURAKAMI DOUBLE 🚨<br><br>Murakami, tied for the MLB lead in homers, was the last qualified player to double! <a href="https://t.co/uBALOX97cA">pic.twitter.com/uBALOX97cA</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2051506953881305305?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">May 5, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Two of his biggest homers came in international play. The first helped Team Japan defeat Team USA in the 2020 Tokyo Summer Olympics baseball finale for its first-ever gold medal. Likewise in the 2023 WBC championship game, Murakami hit a two-run homer to tie the score late as again Japan beat the U.S. for the gold medal.</p><p>This is his pedigree.</p><p>Most Japanese players are creatures of habit, and when Sox manager Will Venable gave him a game off on Tuesday it might have stopped his momentum. He singled when he pinch hit in the sixth inning, but struck out in the eighth. He then followed that with four more whiffs in a row when back in the starting lineup on Wednesday, the Golden Sombrero as you will. He leads the league with 55 whiffs, which is the big knock on his game.</p><p>Murakami didn’t grouse. He was told a day in advance about the night off. But Venable and the coaching staff are obviously feeling their way along on how to handle him.</p><p>“That was his role that night and he played it well,” Venable said. “He had quality at-bats. Everybody has a job to do.”</p><p>No question, though, the contract clock is ticking.</p><p></p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true"
